note: Every panel reads an official statistic at the grain it's reported, attaches a policy lever it bears on, and names the decision that grain can and can't support. Descriptions used to decide, not models used to predict.

lever.lever: 12-month postpartum Medicaid extension (46 states + DC by Mar 2024)
lever.movement_note: Maternal mortality rates for 2023 show the Black rate at 50.3 per 100k births, holding near the 50 mark seen in 2022 (49.5), while the White rate fell to 14.5 from 19.0. These are small annual counts (669 deaths in 2023 versus 817 in 2022 nationally), so single-year movement in any group's rate is noisy and may not be statistically significant. The data detected a wider Black-White ratio in 2023 (roughly 3.5-to-1) than in 2022, consistent with the White average moving downward while the Black rate stayed anchored near 50 — the durable finding is this persistent gap, not the year-to-year tick in either bar. No causal claim is warranted: the pattern is observational, and the data is consistent with, rather than proof of, any driver of the White-rate decline. Notably, the 12-month postpartum Medicaid extension postdates the 2023 outcome window entirely, so it cannot be credited with or blamed for the 2023 figures; it stands as a forward-looking lever aimed at closing the gap, not an explanation of what already happened. Decision: The maternal health program office should treat the Black-White gap as the primary target metric going forward, tracking whether the 12-month postpartum Medicaid extension coincides with narrowing in future years rather than evaluating it against 2023 data.

This panel tracks NAEP mathematics performance across racial/ethnic groups at grade 8, with a grade 4 state-level reference point from Alabama (230 in 2022 to 236 in 2024). At grade 8, Asian/Pacific Islander students moved from 304.0 to 305.0 and White students held flat at 284.0, both within the range expected from sampling variation. Hispanic students fell from 261.0 to 257.0, a move large enough to warrant attention, while Black students registered 252.0 to 251.0, a shift that is not statistically meaningful on its own given normal NAEP sampling error. The more important signal is not any single year's fluctuation but the persistent, non-recovering gap separating Black and Hispanic students from their White and Asian/Pacific Islander peers, a gap that has not closed across this period. NAEP data can detect these patterns and note that they coincided with the tested period, but this observational design does not support any claim that a specific policy or program caused the scores or improved outcomes. Decision: The grade-8 mathematics review board should flag the Black-White and Hispanic-White achievement gaps as a standing priority for targeted instructional support, while treating the single-year Black-group change as statistically inconclusive pending the next assessment cycle.

lever.lever: OTC naloxone (Narcan approved Mar 2023) + pharmacy dispensing scale-up
lever.movement_note: Overdose mortality rates fell across every racial and ethnic group tracked between 2023 and 2024, with the total U.S. overdose death count for 2024 registered at 79,384. American Indian/Alaska Native communities recorded the highest rate in both years, at 65.0 per 100k in 2023 and 51.6 per 100k in 2024, remaining well above every other group despite the decline. Black populations moved from 48.9 to 33.8 per 100k, and White populations from 33.1 to 24.7 per 100k, while Asian and Hispanic populations continued to show the lowest rates, with Asian rates falling from 5.1 to 4.4 per 100k. Even after these declines, a wide gap persists between American Indian/Alaska Native rates and all other groups, indicating the drop has not narrowed underlying disparities. It is important to note that American Indian/Alaska Native overdose death figures are widely understood to be undercounted because of racial misclassification on death certificates, meaning the true burden in this population is likely higher than reported. This pattern of decline coincided with the period under review but should not be read as evidence that any specific program or policy caused the change, given the observational nature of this data. Decision: Public health officials overseeing tribal health and vital-records systems should prioritize corrective action on death-certificate race/ethnicity coding for American Indian/Alaska Native decedents and direct targeted overdose-response resources to this population given its persistently highest and likely undercounted rate.

lever.lever: HRRP penalty on excess 30-day readmissions (ACA section 1886(q); penalties began FY2013, capped at 3% of base DRG payments, 6 conditions)
lever.movement_note: The bars pair 2010 - the year the Affordable Care Act created the Hospital Readmissions Reduction Program (HRRP, Social Security Act section 1886(q)) - against 2016. Raw, unadjusted 30-day unplanned readmissions across all Medicare fee-for-service discharges fell from 16.7 percent to 15.6 percent. That top-line number blends the penalized conditions with everything else, so read the concentration, not just the fall: raw readmissions for the conditions the penalty actually targets declined 13.2 percent over this window against 4.5 percent for conditions the program does not cover - a 2.4 percentage-point drop for targeted conditions versus 0.7 for the rest. That gap is the signal the lever, not a background trend, moved the number. MedPAC tested the obvious confound - that hospitals simply shifted patients into observation stays and emergency visits to dodge the count - and found observation and ED growth almost identical for covered and non-covered conditions, so the drop was not mostly relabeling. Two honest limits travel with this. Coding, not only care: on a risk-adjusted basis heart failure readmissions fall further, from 23.9 to 20.5 percent, but part of that steeper decline reflects hospitals recording more diagnoses after the secondary-diagnosis fields expanded from 9 to 24 in 2011 - a number that improves partly because the measure became easier to satisfy is a Goodhart risk, which is why the raw rates are the more conservative read here. And the raw decline stalled after 2014, so most of the movement is early. On mortality, MedPAC found no adverse effect: risk-adjusted mortality declined across every condition studied, rebutting the fear that fewer readmissions came from turning sick patients away. Decision: the penalty demonstrably moved the targeted number, so the resource read is to hold the incentive where it works while shifting oversight toward the coding drift and the post-2014 plateau, and to weigh MedPAC's own recommendation to fold the HRRP into a broader hospital value program rather than reward a single condition-specific count.

lever.lever: College Scorecard publishes median earnings by specific major at specific school (field-of-study API)
lever.movement_note: Pulled from the College Scorecard field-of-study API — Computer Science (CIP 1107) bachelor's, median earnings four years after completion, Title IV federal-aid recipients only. These six schools are a hand-picked illustration: recognizable programs chosen to zoom in on one contrast, not a representative sample — the dense middle of the field is deliberately off-frame, so read the spread as an illustration of that contrast, not the distribution. Even so it lands. For the same credential in the same field, Carnegie Mellon reports 268,121, MIT 225,141, Stanford 214,907, Harvard 203,169 — while CUNY York reports 73,044 and Fayetteville State 36,164. A CS bachelor's is not one number; across these six, the median runs five- to sevenfold higher at the top than the bottom, and horizon compounds it (Harvard reads 152,251 at one year, 203,169 at four). Two honesty flags. Small cells: at the regional schools the Title-IV completer counts are thin enough that horizons run non-monotonic — Fayetteville reads 50,940 at one year but 36,164 at four — a sign the medians are noisy, not precise. Surface dependence: the same federal source answers differently depending on which door you use — the field-of-study API returns no CS bachelor's figure for Brown at any horizon (its single-year Title-IV completer cohort is below the privacy threshold), yet the public Field of Study web tool shows ~214,000 by pooling two award years into one cohort large enough to clear it. Neither number is wrong; the grain of the query decides whether a number exists at all. Third, selection, not schooling: the figure can't separate the school's effect from who the school admitted — selective programs enroll students who would likely earn more anywhere, so a cross-school gap blends institution with selection and is not a clean read on what the degree itself causes. Decision: the earnings signal is real enough to steer program-level accountability and student guidance — but only if the grain travels with it, because school x CIP x horizon x aid-subgroup is the unit, and a single headline ('CS pays 250k') strips exactly the frame that makes it true. Whether publishing earnings actually changes behavior is separately unsettled: Hurwitz & Smith found a small, advantage-concentrated shift in where students sent scores; later replications found little sorting toward higher-earning schools.

lever.lever: Virginia's Longitudinal Data System links records across five state agencies (K-12, higher ed, employment, community colleges, IT) to measure post-completion wages
lever.movement_note: Virginia's SCHEV report links K-12, higher-ed, and employment records to publish post-completion wages — median annual wage five years out for 2017-18 graduates, measured on 2023 Unemployment-Insurance records (nominal). At the credential median the premium is smaller than the folk model assumes: a four-year bachelor's runs 65,249, an applied-science (AAS) associate 58,393, and a transfer associate 49,444 — so 'higher credential = higher pay' holds on average but only barely, and the distributions overlap heavily. That overlap is the real finding: at these medians a strong applied-science associate can out-earn a weaker bachelor's, so credential tier alone is a poor predictor of individual pay. Two caveats travel with every wage here: it is UI-covered, in-state employment only (out-of-state, federal, military, self-employed, and gig work are invisible), and not every graduate has a reported wage — match rates differ by credential, so the medians describe differently-selected subpopulations. SCHEV itself warns against ranking programs on these wages without adjusting for family wealth, residency, and demographics. Decision: fund and advise by field-and-region outcome, not by credential tier — and open the record-level data (today locked behind data-sharing agreements) so families and funders can interrogate the aggregate instead of trusting it. Built to decide; not built to browse.
